behaviors.description: Content passed to summary-generation includes data from all three sources.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is whether the orchestration layer correctly gathers outputs from all three collectors and hands all of them to the summary step — a module-boundary handoff failure, not a single-function logic bug. REAL-INFRA: The three sources (metrics endpoint, issue tracker API, deployment log) can each be replaced by in-process stubs/mock HTTP clients; no real network or OS primitives are needed to catch an orchestrator that silently drops one source's payload. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the sources is fine here because the behavior under test is the aggregation/forwarding logic, not source response quality. LLM-DEP: This behavior checks what is *passed to* the generation step, not what the LLM produces — no AI/ML output quality involved. MULTI-STEP: Single orchestration step. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network needed; (b) no AI/ML dependency; (c) no chained agentic steps. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points to Integration; output is Integration — no override.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the orchestrator with three stub data-source clients (one for each of metrics, issue tracker, and deployment log), each returning distinct sentinel payloads. Invoke the pipeline up to but not including summary generation (or capture what is passed to the summary step). Assert that the payload forwarded to summary generation contains data from all three stubs and that none is missing or zero-valued.

behaviors.description: When one data source is unavailable, the report is produced with a 'data unavailable' note rather than aborting.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure appears when the orchestration layer catches an error from one collector and decides whether to continue or abort — this is a control-flow decision at a module boundary, not a pure single-function logic test. REAL-INFRA: Source unavailability can be faithfully simulated by a stub that raises a connection exception or returns a 5xx; no real network is required to expose the abort-vs-continue branching bug. MOCK-HIDE: A mock that raises an exception is exactly the right probe here — it is not hiding anything because the failure mode is the orchestrator's response to the exception, not the source's actual network behavior. LLM-DEP: No AI/ML output involved. MULTI-STEP: Single error-handling path. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network needed; (b) no AI/ML dependency; (c) no chained agentic steps. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points to Integration; output is Integration — no override.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the orchestrator with two healthy stub sources and one stub that raises a connection error. Run the full data-collection phase. Assert that the pipeline does not raise an exception, that the output data structure contains 'data unavailable' (or equivalent sentinel) for the failing source's section, and that the other two sections contain valid data.

behaviors.description: The narrative summary accurately represents the key values present in the input data.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: 'Accurately represents' is a quality/faithfulness judgment about LLM-generated prose. A stub or mock LLM would return whatever string we hard-code, making the test trivially pass regardless of whether the real model hallucinates, inverts, or drops key values — the entire failure mode is hidden. Running the test 100 times with a real model may yield different outputs; the correctness criterion is non-deterministic and model-dependent. MOCK-MISS / MOCK-HIDE: Any deterministic replacement for the LLM hides the exact class of failure the behavior describes. REAL-INFRA: No real OS/network primitives required beyond the LLM API call. MULTI-STEP: Single generation step. REJECTION-GATE: Demoting to Integration would require behavior text indicating that a stub LLM suffices to verify narrative accuracy — no such text exists. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points unambiguously to Agentic; output is Agentic — no override.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a known, structured input payload (with specific numeric metrics, issue counts, and deployment records containing distinctive values) to the real summary-generation step using the real LLM. Assert that the produced narrative contains or correctly states the distinctive key values (e.g., specific metric figures, counts), and does not contradict them. Run with a fixed seed or multiple samples to account for non-determinism.

behaviors.description: A complete report — from data collection through email delivery — reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The pipeline chains: (1) data collection from three external sources, (2) LLM-based narrative generation (agentic step), and (3) email delivery to a real inbox. Even if data collection uses stubs, the LLM generation is a real agentic step and verifying the email was 'received correctly' in an inbox requires real email infrastructure. A pipeline that includes at least one agentic step and traverses real external delivery infrastructure constitutes a full agent pipeline per the Workflow definition. LLM-DEP: The summary content in the delivered email depends on real LLM output — a mock would hide generation failures that cause garbled or empty emails. REAL-INFRA: 'Reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly' cannot be verified with a stub SMTP client — the failure mode (email not received, garbled, wrong recipient) only surfaces with real delivery infrastructure. REJECTION-GATE: Demoting to Agentic would require the behavior to say 'verify the generated content' without end-to-end delivery verification; the text explicitly includes inbox receipt. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points to Workflow; output is Workflow — no override.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline end-to-end against stub data sources (returning representative payloads), a real LLM for summary generation, and a real (or sandbox) email service. Assert that a message arrives in the recipient's test inbox, that it contains a non-empty subject and body, and that the body references values present in the stub data. Use a test inbox (e.g., Mailhog or a designated test account) rather than a production address.

behaviors.description: When email delivery is unreachable, the report is saved to a local file and delivery is retried on the next scheduled run.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure spans module boundaries: the email client raises an error → the error handler writes the report to a local file → on a subsequent invocation the pipeline reads the saved file and reattempts delivery. Each handoff is a potential failure point not catchable by a single-function unit test. REAL-INFRA: Email unreachability can be simulated with a stub that raises a connection error. File I/O uses the local filesystem (a real temp directory is appropriate) but no real OS-level primitives beyond standard file operations are needed. 'Next scheduled run' can be reproduced by invoking the pipeline entry point twice in the same test process — no real cron or scheduler infrastructure required. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the filesystem would hide real file-write/read failures, but using a real temp directory avoids this. LLM-DEP: The report is already generated before this behavior's failure mode activates; no AI/ML output quality is at issue. MULTI-STEP: The two sequential pipeline invocations are deterministic, not agentic. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real network/OS primitives beyond local filesystem; (b) no AI/ML dependency; (c) no chained agentic steps. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points to Integration; output is Integration — no override.
behaviors.test_description: Set up the pipeline with a real temp directory and a stub email client configured to fail on the first call and succeed on the second. Invoke the pipeline once (first 'scheduled run'): assert no exception is raised, the email was not sent, and a report file exists in the temp directory. Invoke the pipeline a second time (next 'scheduled run'): assert the email stub was called, the report content matches what was saved, and the local file is cleaned up (or marked delivered) after successful retry.
